---
title: "MD7115 Part 4"
subtitle: "Tree Visualization in Biology"
author: '<br><br>Xiaotao Shen <br> Assistant Professor <br> <a href="http://shen-lab.org"> <i class="fa fa-home fa-fw"></i>&nbsp; shen-lab.org</a><br> <a href="mailto:xiaotao.shen@ntu.edu.sg"><i class="fa fa-paper-plane fa-fw"></i>&nbsp; xiaotao.shen@ntu.edu.sg</a><br>'
institute: "Lee Kong Chian School of Medicine, NTU Singapore"
date: '`r Sys.Date()`'
output:
  xaringan::moon_reader:
    css: [xaringan-themer.css, my-theme.css]
    self_contained: true
    nature:
      ratio: '16:9'
      countdown: false
      titleSlideClass: [center, middle]
      countIncrementalSlides: false
    seal: true
    yolo: false
---

layout: true
background-image: url(images/lkc_logo.png)
background-position: 100% 0%
background-size: 20%

<div class="my-footer"><span>Xiaotao Shen/www.shen-lab.org</span></div>

---

```{r setup, include=FALSE}
knitr::opts_chunk$set(
  echo = TRUE,
  eval = FALSE,
  message = FALSE,
  warning = FALSE,
  cache = FALSE,
  dpi = 72
)
```

# Part 4 goals

In this section, we will:

1. Understand what a biological tree represents
2. Read a microbiome phyloseq object
3. Generate a basic tree
4. Add taxonomic or phenotypic annotations
5. Interpret a layered tree figure

---

# Why trees matter in biology

- taxonomy is hierarchical
- evolutionary relationships are hierarchical
- lineage relationships are hierarchical
- tree plots let us show structure and annotation together

---

# Example: microbiome taxonomic tree

.pull-left[
Nodes can represent:

- kingdom
- phylum
- class
- order
- family
- genus
]

.pull-right[
<img src="https://raw.githubusercontent.com/jaspershen-lab/MD7115-NTU/main/3_materials/images/cell_host_microbe.png" alt="Microbiome tree" width="80%">
]

---

# Packages

```{r}
library(readr)
library(tidyverse)
library(phyloseq)
library(microbiomeViz)
library(ggtree)
library(ggtreeExtra)
library(ggnewscale)
```

---

# Data for the tree showcase

```{r}
personalized_score <- readr::read_csv("2_demo_data/personalized_score.csv")
load("2_demo_data/microbiome_data.RData")
```

Inputs:

- one `phyloseq` object
- one table of additional metadata or scores

---

# What is inside a phyloseq object?

A `phyloseq` object may contain:

- OTU or ASV abundance table
- taxonomy table
- sample metadata
- phylogenetic tree

This makes it convenient for integrated microbiome analysis.

---

# Parse the tree

```{r}
tree <-
  microbiomeViz::parsePhyloseq(
    physeq = microbiome_data,
    use_abundance = FALSE,
    node.size.scale = 0,
    node.size.offset = 0
  )

tree
```

---

# Draw a basic tree

```{r}
raw_plot <-
  tree.backbone(
    tree = tree,
    size = 0.3,
    shape = 16,
    layout = "circular",
    fill = "black",
    color = "black"
  )

raw_plot
```

---

# Add taxonomic node classes

```{r}
raw_plot$data$nodeClass2 <- as.character(raw_plot$data$nodeClass)
raw_plot$data$nodeClass2[is.na(raw_plot$data$nodeClass2)] <- "Root"
raw_plot$data$nodeClass2[raw_plot$data$nodeClass2 == "k"] <- "Kingdom"
raw_plot$data$nodeClass2[raw_plot$data$nodeClass2 == "p"] <- "Phylum"
raw_plot$data$nodeClass2[raw_plot$data$nodeClass2 == "c"] <- "Class"
raw_plot$data$nodeClass2[raw_plot$data$nodeClass2 == "o"] <- "Order"
raw_plot$data$nodeClass2[raw_plot$data$nodeClass2 == "f"] <- "Family"
raw_plot$data$nodeClass2[raw_plot$data$nodeClass2 == "g"] <- "Genus"
```

---

# Add node layers

```{r}
raw_plot +
  ggtree::geom_point2(aes(color = nodeClass2), size = 1.2, show.legend = FALSE)
```

This immediately makes the hierarchy easier to read.

---

# Add labels for the tips

```{r}
raw_plot$data$label2 <-
  raw_plot$data$label %>%
  stringr::str_split("__") %>%
  purrr::map_chr(~ ifelse(length(.x) >= 2, .x[2], NA_character_))

raw_plot$data$label2[as.character(raw_plot$data$nodeClass) != "g"] <- NA
```

Tip labels should be selective and legible.

---

# Add external annotation

Typical annotations for a tree include:

- abundance
- enrichment score
- body site
- significance mark
- taxonomy color

This is where `ggtreeExtra` becomes very useful.

---

# Prepare a simple annotation table

```{r}
annotation_df <-
  personalized_score %>%
  dplyr::select(genus, class, fc1) %>%
  tidyr::pivot_wider(names_from = class, values_from = fc1)

head(annotation_df)
```

---

# Add a heatmap layer

```{r}
ggtree::gheatmap(
  p = raw_plot,
  data = annotation_df,
  offset = 0.2,
  width = 0.2,
  colnames = TRUE
)
```

Conceptually, this is still one tree plus one aligned metadata panel.

---

# A good tree figure usually has three parts

1. backbone structure
2. taxonomic or lineage annotation
3. external data layer

If all three are clear, the plot becomes biologically useful.

---

# What students should understand

- the tree encodes hierarchy
- tip-level metadata can be aligned to the tree
- added layers should support interpretation
- more layers do not always mean a better figure

---

# Transition

We have now built one network view and one tree view.

The final step is to discuss interpretation and common mistakes.
